topic: Two-pole geomagnetic model — toroidal ring-magnet self-contradiction
our_argument_summary: The dome's V51.0 'Closed Toroidal Ovoid' architecture fits B(r) with two exponentials centred on opposite poles (A=62,376 nT, C=64,852 nT; λ=8,619 km). Flux conservation around the claimed ring-magnet circuit — pole-aperture area ≈ 7.85×10^5 km² vs sub-terrestrial return area ≈ 1.26×10^9 km² — forces B_south ≈ 39 nT, producing a ~1,660:1 internal contradiction with the dome's own fitted 64,852 nT. We additionally argue that the two coefficients are fitted to observed magnetic-pole field strengths rather than derived from dome geometry, and that the dome's 20% RMS (~10–13 kNT) is ~200× worse than IGRF-13. Verdict: Self-Contradicted.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitor.py is published in the raw-text snapshot, and the evidence text asserts 'monitor.py does not track WIN-053, and no script computes B(r).' The dome presents B(r) = 62,376·e^(-r_N/8619) + 64,852·e^(-r_S/8619) nT as static prose; there is no automated validation pipeline for this WIN.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Display only — B(r) coefficients A, C and λ are stated as fit outputs. No runtime computation compares B(r) predictions against any observed data source.
data_sources_used: None live. Coefficients appear to be fit to WMM/IGRF pole-strength values post-hoc.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Registered PRED-TOROID-002 (2026-04-04) gives ±15% deviation on λ as a falsification threshold; the 'confirmation' date 2026-03-23 predates the registration date (retrodiction).
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None found. The 'no monitor.py coverage' claim in the current text is consistent with our snapshot. If the dome later adds a monitor.py entry, this should be re-checked.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py domain is present in the raw-text scrape, and our text states 'monitor.py does not track WIN-053, and no script computes B(r).' The dome presents the B(r) equation as static prose on the predictions page. If a future monitor.py adds geomagnetic tracking, this tag must be revisited.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard physics (IGRF-13 / WMM2025) already models the dipolar + multipolar geomagnetic field from first-principles dynamo theory and fits ~195 Gauss coefficients to ~50 nT RMS globally. The dome's two-parameter radial exponential achieves ~20% RMS (~10–13 kNT — ~200× worse) while reusing WMM pole-strength magnitudes as its fitted amplitudes. This is renaming a well-modelled phenomenon ('dipolar main field') as 'aetheric ring-magnet flux.'
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Coefficients A=62,376 nT and C=64,852 nT track the observed pole field strengths (North Magnetic Pole ~54–56 kNT, South Magnetic Pole ~65–67 kNT per WMM2025 and IGRF-13), both published for decades. No derivation from dome parameters exists. The prediction registration (PRED-TOROID-002, 2026-04-04) post-dates the 'confirmation' date (2026-03-23) — the 'prediction' was registered after the data was used to fit the formula.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome provides no derivation of A, C, or λ from disc_radius, firmament_height H(r), or any other dome-specific geometric primitive. λ=8,619 km is shared with H(r) but is not itself derived — it is a fit parameter of H(r). The B(r) functional form (two-sided exponential) is chosen to fit observed pole strengths, not derived from the claimed toroidal topology (whose textbook analogue, a wound solenoid, would give B∝1/r — and whose general closed-loop form is constrained by flux conservation, which the fit violates).

holes_found.description: TLDR FACTUAL ERROR — pole location contradicts the evidence. The current tldr_evidence says: 'Instead, the dome claims two similar-strength poles at the same location (north).' But the evidence text unambiguously places the two poles at OPPOSITE ends of the disc (A is the north-pole coefficient with B peaking at r_N=0; C is the south-pole coefficient with B peaking at r_S=0, i.e., r_N=D). A non-specialist reading the TLDR before expanding will come away believing the dome places both poles at the north — which is not what the dome claims and not what the detail_evidence argues. Per the TLDR review severity guidance, factual errors in TLDRs are 'major' because readers may never expand. The evidence-side rebuttal would be weaker if, in fact, the dome put both poles at the north (then the dome's own formula wouldn't produce a two-pole field).
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite tldr_evidence to accurately describe the geometry. Suggested: 'A closed-loop ring magnet must conserve flux — the small pole aperture and the huge return channel force one pole to be ~1,600× weaker than the other. Instead, the dome's fitted formula places two similar-strength peaks at opposite ends of the disc (A=62,376 nT at the north, C=64,852 nT at the south). That violates flux conservation in the dome's own topology.' This preserves the punch without the 'same location (north)' misstatement.

holes_found.description: TWO BROKEN SECTION CROSS-REFERENCES. The detail_evidence contains two §-references that are factually wrong in the V6-restructured numbering: (1) '(see Section 6.8: Globe Values Relabeled)' — §6.8 is actually 'The WIN-053 Single-Pole Collapse' per sections.json/part6; the section titled 'Globe Values Relabeled as Dome Predictions' is §6.4. The label is wrong AND the number is wrong. (2) 'See also §3.5.8 for the parallel single-pole collapse affecting WIN-012 and the κ coupling constant.' Per monitor/v6-restructure-map.json, §3.5.8 (old) → §6.8 (new) — but §6.8 is 'The WIN-053 Single-Pole Collapse' (this very WIN), NOT a section about WIN-012. There is no dedicated 'WIN-012 single-pole collapse' section in the new structure; the κ denominator / single-pole collapse discussion lives inside Part 4 (e.g., §4.2.4 four E-PRED tests) and Part 6/7 in scattered form. Both cross-references are dead links that would mislead a reader following the self-cross-reference chain. The c3 review flagged the second one (as 'MISSING CROSS-REFERENCE TO SECTION 3.5.8') and it was never fixed; the first one ('Section 6.8: Globe Values Relabeled') appears to be a fresh error introduced in the recent rewrite.
holes_found.recommendation: Change '(see Section 6.8: Globe Values Relabeled)' to '(see Section 6.4: Globe Values Relabeled as Dome Predictions).' For the §3.5.8 reference, either (a) drop it (the current text already covers WIN-012/κ context via the flux-conservation argument), or (b) replace with a specific new-numbering target that actually discusses the WIN-012 κ-denominator collapse (§4.2.4 E-PRED-C 0.0 µGal denominator, or the relevant Part 4 internal-contradictions subsection). Do NOT translate §3.5.8 → §6.8 mechanically — that lands on the self-referential WIN-053 section, which is circular and unhelpful.

holes_found.description: STILL OPEN — 'SUB-TERRESTRIAL RETURN CHANNEL' DEFENSE NOT EXPLICITLY PREEMPTED. c3 first raised this (rating 3/5); c4 logged it as H2. The strongest sympathetic dome defence is: 'the 64,852 nT south-pole coefficient refers to the SURFACE re-entry zone above the Sump, not the return-channel field itself; the return-channel field is small (as flux conservation requires), but the surface field near the south aperture can remain large because the return re-concentrates into a second small aperture symmetrically mirroring the north one.' Under this reading, both poles are small apertures (each ~7.85×10^5 km²) and the 'return' is broadly distributed through the sub-terrestrial disc, so flux conservation does not constrain surface magnitudes. Our text mentions the return area in the ratio calculation but never explicitly says why this symmetric-aperture rescue fails. It does fail, but the argument needs to be made: if both apertures are small and symmetric, the dome's own fit would need B_north = B_south exactly (the circuit is symmetric by construction), whereas A=62,376 nT and C=64,852 nT differ by 4% — and more importantly, the FITTED magnitudes match the observed WMM2025 magnetic-pole strengths (which themselves differ because Earth's geodynamo is NOT symmetric). So the dome is simultaneously claiming (a) a symmetric closed toroidal circuit and (b) fitted magnitudes that match the globe's genuinely-asymmetric dipole. Those are incompatible.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a ~3-sentence preemption paragraph after the sub-terrestrial return-area calculation: 'A dome defender might argue the 64,852 nT coefficient describes the surface re-entry aperture above the Sump (symmetric with the north aperture), not the return-channel field itself. But that rescue requires A = C by construction (two identical apertures in a closed symmetric circuit), while the fitted values differ by 4% AND match the observationally-asymmetric WMM2025 pole strengths — which are asymmetric because Earth's real geodynamo is asymmetric, not because of any dome-predicted mechanism. The symmetric-aperture rescue is incompatible with the fitted coefficients.'

holes_found.description: TLDR IMPRECISION — 'simplifies to a single exponential' is structurally wrong. Current tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict both assert: 'The formula also simplifies to a single exponential — it is one pole pretending to be two.' This is technically false. With A ≈ C (they differ by 4%), B(r_N) = A·[exp(-r_N/λ) + exp(-(D-r_N)/λ)] = 2A·exp(-D/(2λ))·cosh((r_N - D/2)/λ). That is a hyperbolic cosine — a U-shaped function with a minimum at the disc centre and maxima at both poles — which is STRUCTURALLY DIFFERENT from a single exponential (monotonic decay). The 'one pole pretending to be two' phrase is a rhetorical flourish that doesn't match the mathematics: the formula genuinely produces two peaks. A defender who does the algebra will find the TLDR wrong on a checkable technical point, which weakens our credibility disproportionately to the argumentative gain. This probably is not the main thrust we want — the main thrust is the flux-conservation violation, which the TLDR already carries.
holes_found.recommendation: Drop the 'simplifies to a single exponential' sentence from tldr_evidence and tldr_verdict, OR replace with an accurate simplification: 'with A≈C the formula reduces to a cosh with two peaks — mathematically two poles, but structurally identical to the globe's observed dipole strengths.' The flux-conservation violation is the load-bearing argument; do not risk it on a wrong algebra claim.

holes_found.description: TLDR 'ZERO FITTED PARAMETERS' CLAIM ATTRIBUTION — the tldr_verdict says 'the zero fitted parameters claim ignores two unexplained coefficients,' but the detail_evidence for WIN-053 never quotes the dome saying 'zero fitted parameters' for this WIN specifically. The phrase IS used by the dome (per §6.1 'Zero Fitted Parameters — The Hidden Parameters'), but our WIN-053 text does not source it in context. A defender can credibly respond: 'we never claimed zero fitted parameters for B(r) specifically — that claim was about the aggregate model.' This defence would be wrong (the dome does assert aggregate 'zero fit' and B(r)'s coefficients are two of the hidden fits that §6.1 catalogues), but because the TLDR raises it without sourcing, we look sloppy.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) drop the 'zero fitted parameters' line from the tldr_verdict (the flux-conservation point is enough), or (b) add a sourced sentence to detail_evidence: 'The dome's §6.1 framing that ECM has zero fitted parameters is directly contradicted by A, C, and λ in the B(r) fit (see §6.1 catalogue of hidden parameters).'

holes_found.description: STILL OPEN — no 3,000 km aperture sensitivity row (c4 H6). The current text already does the sensitivity for 500 km (1,660:1) and 1,000 km (415:1). Adding a 3,000 km row would close the 'what if the aperture is huge' quibble for good: area π·9×10^6 km² ≈ 2.83×10^7 km²; area ratio 1.26×10^9 / 2.83×10^7 ≈ 44.5; B_south = 62,376 / 44.5 ≈ 1,402 nT; discrepancy 64,852 / 1,402 ≈ 46×. Still catastrophic — a 46× self-contradiction at a generous aperture five times the size our calculation assumes.
holes_found.recommendation: Append to the existing sensitivity parenthetical: '...and even at a generous 3,000 km aperture (π·9×10^6 km² ≈ 2.83×10^7 km²), the area ratio remains ~45:1 and B_south would be ~1,400 nT — still ~46× below the fitted value. The self-contradiction survives any plausible aperture size.'

holes_found.description: STILL OPEN — flux direction (source vs sink) not discussed (c4 H7 / c3 H5). Flux conservation constrains both magnitude AND direction. The dome's fitted B(r) = A·exp(-r_N/λ) + C·exp(-r_S/λ) has both terms POSITIVE — i.e., both poles are treated as sources of the same sign. In a genuine ring-magnet toroidal circuit, one pole is a source (field emerging) and the other is a sink (field re-entering); they have OPPOSITE signed polarity. The fitted formula encodes no polarity reversal between north and south, so even setting magnitudes aside, it's topologically a monopole-pair, not a dipole. This is a second independent violation of the claimed topology.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence to the flux-conservation paragraph: 'The fit also encodes both poles as positive-signed sources (A, C both positive). A real toroidal ring-magnet has one source and one sink with opposite polarity — the dome's formula is not just quantitatively wrong about flux, it is topologically a monopole pair, not a dipole.'

holes_found.description: STILL OPEN — Sub-H(r) formula δ = 6,371 km unsourced (c4 H4). The detail_extra says: 'the subterranean cavity depth is given by Sub-H(r) = H(r) × (1 − e^(−r/δ)) with δ = 6,371 km — notably, this is Earth's mean radius, a globe-derived constant with no derivation from dome geometry.' The observation is strong, but no dome-page citation or URL is given. A defender can ask 'where on the dome site does this formula appear?' and we do not point to a source.
holes_found.recommendation: Add an href or inline citation (e.g., a link to the model.txt or predictions page URL where Sub-H is stated). If the formula is only discussed in dome-author commentary and not on-site, note that explicitly ('per [dome author statement dated YYYY-MM-DD]').

holes_found.description: '~50 nT RMS globally' IGRF-13 FIT ACCURACY — imprecise. IGRF-13 main-field coefficients have substantial uncertainties from both measurement noise and secular-variation extrapolation. Typical model residuals against observatory data run ~10–50 nT at source epochs and grow to ~100 nT away from source epochs (the 2020 model performance degrades by ~5 nT/yr until the 2025 update). '~50 nT RMS globally (~0.1% of typical field strength)' is in the generous-to-representative range and is fine for a prose argument, but a numerically rigorous defender could ask for a source.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: cite IGRF-13 performance numbers directly (e.g., Alken et al. 2021, Earth, Planets and Space 73:49, DOI:10.1186/s40623-020-01288-x). Low priority since the ~200× gap is robust even if we pessimise IGRF residuals to 100 nT.

stronger_arguments.description: POLARITY-SIGN ARGUMENT (extending H7). The most decisive single-sentence rebuttal is structural, not numerical: the dome's fitted B(r) is a sum of two positive exponentials with no sign reversal, which is the functional form of a MONOPOLE PAIR (both sources), not a DIPOLE (source + sink). The physical constraint ∇·B = 0 (no magnetic monopoles) means any closed-loop field must reverse sign somewhere between the poles. The dome's fit never does — it stays positive everywhere. This alone, independent of flux magnitudes, disqualifies the formula as a description of a closed magnetic circuit. Combining with the magnitude argument gives a two-independent-failure critique: (a) magnitudes incompatible with flux conservation, (b) polarity incompatible with ∇·B = 0.
sources: Jackson, Classical Electrodynamics, §5.9 (multipole expansion); Griffiths, Introduction to Electrodynamics, §5.2 (∇·B = 0).

stronger_arguments.description: HISTORIC PARAMETER-REUSE ARGUMENT. λ = 8,619 km is the SAME parameter used in H(r) for firmament height (fit from Polaris-elevation observations) AND in B(r) for magnetic field. No physical principle links firmament height to magnetic field; in globe physics these are unrelated scales (atmospheric scale height ~8 km; geomagnetic decay scale ~R_Earth=6,371 km). The coincidence that a single aetheric λ governs both is either (a) a deep unification claim that requires derivation, or (b) a cosmetic re-use to boost the 'zero fitted parameters' count. The dome provides no derivation, so (b) is the operative reading. This strengthens the post_hoc tag substantially.
sources: Internal: §6.1 'Zero Fitted Parameters — The Hidden Parameters' already catalogues λ as one of the reused constants. Reference it from WIN-053 explicitly.

stronger_arguments.description: ALTERNATIVE FIT TEST. A defender could challenge: 'maybe ANY two-parameter exponential fit would achieve ~20% RMS globally, so this does not demonstrate anything specific to the dome.' Run the comparison: fit B(r) = a·exp(-r/L) + b·exp(-(D-r)/L) with a,b,L free to the WMM2025 field at geographic latitudes. If the resulting (a,b,L) are close to (62376, 64852, 8619), the dome fit is just a globe-data fit in disguise. If they are far off, the dome has chosen specific parameters we can challenge. Either outcome strengthens our case — the former shows circularity, the latter shows the dome values are not even a best-fit to the data they appear to describe.
sources: WMM2025 grid data: https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/world-magnetic-model; fit with scipy.optimize.curve_fit.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome correctly identifies that a single-pole model (V50.6's monopolar form) cannot explain a broadly dipolar geomagnetic field with comparable strength at both geographic poles. The architectural move from monopole to toroidal-ring topology in V51.0 IS a genuine physical improvement in the sense that it at least tries to admit two-sided field structure. Re-using λ=8,619 km across H(r) and B(r) is also an attempt at parameter parsimony — a kind of unification impulse — rather than free multiplication of fit parameters.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The execution introduces a new quantitative self-contradiction (1,660:1 flux mismatch) that V50.6's monopole model did not have — a monopole has no closed loop, so flux conservation cannot be violated by construction. The architectural 'fix' made the model worse on its own internal consistency. And the parameter-parsimony instinct is undone by the fact that λ was originally fit to Polaris observations (not derived from first principles) and is reused without any theoretical link between firmament-height and field-strength scales. So the kernel of truth ('admit two-sided field structure') is correct motivation; the execution violates its own geometry.

tldr_review.tldr_evidence_status: major factual error — 'at the same location (north)' contradicts the evidence; also 'simplifies to a single exponential' is structurally wrong (it is a cosh).
tldr_review.tldr_verdict_status: moderate — 'zero fitted parameters' framing unsourced in this WIN's detail text; 'simplifies to a single exponential' carries the same math error.

deeper_analysis: The c4 decider successfully patched H1 (boilerplate removal from verdict_text), H3 (IGRF-13 coefficient count: 195 confirmed), and H5 (north pole field strength: 54–56 kNT confirmed against WMM2025). What remains is (a) the TLDR pair that was apparently written before the verdict_text rewrite and is now out-of-sync with the evidence body, (b) two broken section cross-references (§6.8 mis-titled as 'Globe Values Relabeled' when it is actually 'The WIN-053 Single-Pole Collapse'; §3.5.8 is stale V6-old-numbering that maps to §6.8 — which is circular), and (c) the pending sensitivity/direction/return-channel preemptions from c3/c4 that never landed. None of these change the verdict: the 1,660:1 flux-conservation violation and the post-hoc coefficient fit remain load-bearing, and 'Self-Contradicted' is accurate. But the TLDR/cross-reference errors WILL be weaponised by a careful defender — they are low-hanging rhetorical targets that cost us credibility for zero argumentative benefit. On the positive side, the major rewrite materially improved the verdict_text. The category-error boilerplate is gone; the post-hoc framing is cleaner; the IGRF-13/WMM2025 numbers check out. The evidence text's arithmetic is internally consistent (500 km aperture → 1,600:1 ratio → 39 nT → 1,660:1 discrepancy, all verified). The Griffiths nuance paragraph correctly concedes that the toroidal-solenoid 1/r form does not directly constrain the dome's aetheric flow path while preserving the flux-conservation argument as the stronger claim. The single highest-leverage improvement would be extending the flux-conservation argument to polarity-sign: a fit of two positive exponentials cannot describe a closed magnetic loop (it is topologically a monopole pair, not a dipole). That argument is independent of the magnitude mismatch and would survive any 'maybe the aperture is huge' or 'maybe the return is narrow' defence. c4 H7 raised this; c3 H5 raised this; it should finally land in c5 patching. Finally, the content-security scan: I searched the WIN-053 text for prompt-injection patterns ('ignore previous,' 'system message,' 'update your review') and found none. The dome-side content I examined was all scientific/numerical prose with no meta-instructions.

citations_verified: Griffiths, Introduction to Electrodynamics, §5.3.3 Example 5.12 (toroidal solenoid B = μ₀NI/(2πs)) — textbook reference, verified correct.
citations_failed: '(see Section 6.8: Globe Values Relabeled)' — INTERNAL CROSS-REFERENCE WRONG. §6.8 in sections.json/part6 is titled 'The WIN-053 Single-Pole Collapse,' not 'Globe Values Relabeled.' The section actually titled 'Globe Values Relabeled as Dome Predictions' is §6.4.

'§3.5.8 for the parallel single-pole collapse affecting WIN-012' — STALE V6-OLD NUMBERING. Per monitor/v6-restructure-map.json, §3.5.8 → §6.8, which is the WIN-053 single-pole section (self-referential). No section in the new structure specifically covers WIN-012 κ-denominator collapse as its own subsection.
citations_unchecked: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.epsl.2018.12.019 — DOI returns HTTP 200 via redirect but final landing page title was not captured (Redirecting page). Based on prefix 10.1016/j.epsl.2018.12.019 (Elsevier EPSL, 2019 volume), this is most likely Tarduno et al. 2019 'Antiquity of the South Atlantic Anomaly' or similar SAA-origins paper — consistent with the context. Recommend explicit title/author confirmation on next run.
